title: Cloverleaf rolls
subtitle: Soft enriched dinner rolls with a three-segment cloverleaf top
excerpt: An enriched white dough portioned into small balls, three per muffin cup, and baked until the segments merge at the base and the top shows three distinct domes. Soft, slightly sweet, and pull-apart.
sourceNotes: Cloverleaf rolls are an American dinner roll form documented in domestic cookery from the late nineteenth century. The technique of placing three small dough balls in a muffin cup so they rise and merge into a three-lobed top is described in Fannie Farmer's Boston Cooking-School Cook Book (1896) and appears in American domestic cookery throughout the early twentieth century. The enriched dough base (milk, butter, egg) is identical to the Parker House roll tradition.

content.text: Cloverleaf rolls are enriched white rolls shaped by portioning the dough into 36 small pieces and placing three per muffin cup. During proofing and baking, the three pieces merge at the base and the tops swell into distinct domes. The enriched dough (milk, butter, and egg alongside the flour) keeps them soft for two days without going stale. The 60% hydration makes the dough smooth and workable: firm enough to shape cleanly.
Mix, knead, and bulk ferment, 1 hour

Mix the {{strong-white-flour}}, {{fast-action-yeast}}, {{fine-salt}}, and {{caster-sugar}} in a large bowl. Make a well and add the {{whole-milk}} and {{whole-eggs}}. Mix into a shaggy dough, then add the softened {{unsalted-butter}} and knead by hand for 10 minutes, or 6 minutes in a stand mixer on medium, until smooth and elastic. The dough should feel soft and slightly tacky. Cover and leave to rise at room temperature until doubled, about 1 hour.

Lightly grease a 12-hole muffin tin. Divide the dough into 36 equal pieces (about 18 g each). Roll each piece into a tight ball. Place three balls per muffin cup, touching. Cover with oiled cling film and prove until the balls are puffy and fill the cups, about 45 minutes.
Heat the oven to 200°C (180°C fan). Bake for 15-18 minutes until the tops are deep golden. Brush immediately with the melted butter. Leave in the tin for 5 minutes, then turn out and serve warm.
